title: IMCI general danger signs (under-5)
text: A child with any general danger sign needs urgent referral: unable to drink or breastfeed, vomits everything, convulsions (current or during this illness), lethargic or unconscious. Assess every sick child for these before anything else.
source: WHO Integrated Management of Childhood Illness — Chart booklet

title: Fast-breathing thresholds for childhood pneumonia
text: Count breaths for one minute. Fast breathing = 50 or more breaths/min for an infant 2-11 months, and 40 or more breaths/min for a child 12 months up to 5 years. Fast breathing or chest indrawing indicates pneumonia and warrants antibiotics.
source: WHO Integrated Management of Childhood Illness — Chart booklet

title: ORS + zinc for childhood diarrhoea
text: Treat diarrhoea with low-osmolarity oral rehydration solution (ORS) plus zinc for 10-14 days: 20 mg/day for children, 10 mg/day for infants under 6 months. Zinc reduces the duration and severity of the episode and the likelihood of recurrence over the following 2-3 months.
source: Clinical management of acute diarrhoea: WHO/UNICEF joint statement (WHO/FCH/CAH/04.7)

title: Metformin for type 2 diabetes
text: Metformin is the first-line oral medicine for type 2 diabetes in adults and is on the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines. Typical adult dosing starts at 500 mg once or twice daily with meals and is titrated up; it is contraindicated in significant renal impairment.
source: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines, 23rd list

title: Amoxicillin for non-severe childhood pneumonia
text: Oral amoxicillin is first-line for non-severe (fast-breathing) pneumonia in children, having replaced oral cotrimoxazole. Refer urgently if there are general danger signs or chest indrawing with severe features.
source: Revised WHO classification and treatment of childhood pneumonia at health facilities

title: Hypertension diagnostic threshold
text: Hypertension is generally diagnosed when office systolic blood pressure is 140 mmHg or higher, or diastolic is 90 mmHg or higher, on more than one occasion. Confirm with repeat measurements; isolated elevated readings are not sufficient.
source: WHO Guideline for the pharmacological treatment of hypertension in adults

title: Preferred first-line ART (dolutegravir-based)
text: WHO's preferred first-line antiretroviral therapy for adults is a dolutegravir (DTG)-based regimen, typically tenofovir + lamivudine + dolutegravir (the single-pill TLD). Dolutegravir is preferred for both first- and second-line for its efficacy, tolerability, and high genetic barrier to resistance.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: Stavudine (d4T) is no longer recommended
text: Stavudine (d4T)-based regimens are no longer recommended. WHO advised phasing out stavudine because of frequent, often irreversible mitochondrial toxicity — peripheral neuropathy, lipoatrophy, and lactic acidosis; tenofovir (TDF) or zidovudine (AZT) are the recommended nucleoside backbones instead.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: Treat-all: start ART regardless of CD4
text: WHO recommends antiretroviral therapy for everyone diagnosed with HIV, regardless of CD4 count or WHO clinical stage ('treat all'), and as early as possible after diagnosis.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: WHO clinical staging of HIV
text: WHO clinical staging grades HIV disease severity from stage 1 (asymptomatic) through stage 2 (mild symptoms), stage 3 (advanced — e.g. significant weight loss, persistent fever or diarrhoea, pulmonary TB) to stage 4 (severe / AIDS-defining illness). It is used alongside CD4 to guide management.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: Role of the CD4 count
text: The CD4 count reflects immune status in HIV; lower counts mean higher risk of opportunistic infection. A CD4 below 200 cells/mm3 indicates advanced HIV disease and the need for opportunistic-infection prophylaxis. ART is started regardless of the CD4 count.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: ART adherence
text: Sustained high adherence to antiretroviral therapy is essential to achieve and maintain viral suppression and to prevent drug resistance; adherence assessment and support are part of routine HIV care at every visit.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: TB in people living with HIV
text: Tuberculosis is the leading cause of death among people living with HIV. WHO recommends routine TB symptom screening at every visit and TB preventive treatment (e.g. isoniazid preventive therapy) for people with HIV who are unlikely to have active TB.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: Co-trimoxazole preventive therapy
text: Co-trimoxazole preventive therapy is recommended for people living with HIV who have advanced disease or a low CD4 count, to prevent Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) and other opportunistic infections.
source: WHO Consolidated guidelines on HIV prevention, testing, treatment, service delivery and monitoring

title: The CIEL concept dictionary
text: The CIEL (Columbia International eHealth Laboratory) dictionary is the de-facto reference concept dictionary for OpenMRS; most new implementations start from it. It maps concepts to international terminologies including ICD-10, SNOMED CT, LOINC, RxNorm, and CVX.

title: Open Concept Lab (OCL)
text: Open Concept Lab (OCL, openconceptlab.org) is a cloud terminology platform that publishes curated subsets of source dictionaries such as CIEL; OpenMRS implementations subscribe to a managed concept collection via the OCL module.

title: OpenMRS information model (obs / encounter / visit)
text: In OpenMRS an Observation (obs) is one piece of information recorded about a patient at a point in time, with a Concept as its 'question' and a typed value (numeric, coded, text, or date). Observations belong to an Encounter (a single patient-provider interaction with a type, datetime, location and provider), and encounters belong to a Visit.

title: OpenMRS concept dictionary
text: The OpenMRS concept dictionary defines every question and answer that can be recorded — each concept's name, code, datatype and class. A patient's chart is largely a set of coded observations whose clinical meaning comes from the concept dictionary (commonly CIEL).
